event: Australopithecus afarensis hominins leave the Laetoli footprints preserved in volcanic ash, providing the earliest direct evidence of bipedal walking.
Early hominins including Homo habilis and Paranthropus boisei inhabit Olduvai Gorge, leaving some of the oldest known stone tools and fossils of human ancestors.
Cushitic-speaking pastoralists migrate into the region, introducing cattle herding and early agriculture to the East African interior.
Southern Cushitic and later Khoisan hunter-gatherer communities populate the highlands, with rock art and pastoral cultures developing across the savanna.
Bantu-speaking peoples migrate into the region bringing ironworking and intensive agriculture, gradually becoming the dominant population.
Swahili coastal settlements flourish as Bantu communities trade with Arab and Persian merchants across the Indian Ocean, giving rise to the Swahili language and culture.
The trading town of Kilwa Kisiwani emerges on the southern coast and grows into one of the most powerful city-states of the Swahili Coast.
Kilwa gains control of the lucrative gold trade from Sofala, becoming the wealthiest and most influential Swahili sultanate.
The traveler Ibn Battuta visits Kilwa and describes it as one of the most beautiful and well-built cities in the world.
Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama reaches the East African coast en route to India, opening the era of European intrusion into Swahili affairs.
Portuguese forces sack and capture Kilwa and Mombasa, imposing control over the Swahili Coast and disrupting Indian Ocean trade networks.
Omani Arabs, allied with the Swahili, expel the Portuguese from the northern coast, ending Portuguese dominance over the region.
Sultan Said bin Sultan of Oman moves his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar, making the island a center of the clove and slave trades.
Zanzibar becomes a separate sultanate under Majid bin Said, ruling the East African coast and inland caravan trade routes.
British explorers Richard Burton and John Hanning Speke reach Lake Tanganyika, intensifying European interest in the East African interior.
Under British pressure, Sultan Barghash of Zanzibar signs a treaty abolishing the slave trade and closes the Zanzibar slave market.
Carl Peters' German East Africa Company secures treaties with local chiefs, and Germany declares a protectorate over the mainland during the Scramble for Africa.
The Anglo-German Heligoland Treaty defines colonial boundaries, placing Zanzibar under British protection and confirming German control of the mainland.
The German Empire takes direct control of the colony of German East Africa from the German East Africa Company.
The Maji Maji Rebellion erupts against German rule, and its brutal suppression and resulting famine kill an estimated 250,000 to 300,000 Africans.
British and allied forces invade German East Africa during World War I, while German commander Lettow-Vorbeck wages a prolonged guerrilla campaign.
Following Germany's defeat in World War I, the mainland becomes the British-administered League of Nations mandate territory of Tanganyika.
The Tanganyika African Association is founded, providing an early platform for African political and welfare organization.
Tanganyika becomes a United Nations Trust Territory under British administration after World War II.
Julius Nyerere transforms the African Association into the Tanganyika African National Union (TANU), launching the drive for independence.
Tanganyika gains independence from Britain on 9 December with Julius Nyerere as prime minister, joining the United Nations the same year.
Tanganyika becomes a republic with Julius Nyerere elected as its first president.
Zanzibar gains independence from Britain as a constitutional monarchy under its sultan.
The Zanzibar Revolution overthrows the Arab sultanate, and in April Tanganyika and Zanzibar unite to form the United Republic of Tanzania.
Nyerere issues the Arusha Declaration, committing Tanzania to African socialism (ujamaa), self-reliance, and the nationalization of major industries.
The capital is officially designated to move from Dar es Salaam to the more central Dodoma.
TANU and Zanzibar's Afro-Shirazi Party merge to form Chama Cha Mapinduzi (CCM), entrenching one-party rule.
Ugandan forces under Idi Amin invade and annex the Kagera region, triggering the Uganda-Tanzania War.
Tanzanian forces and Ugandan exiles capture Kampala and overthrow Idi Amin, ending the Uganda-Tanzania War.
Julius Nyerere steps down voluntarily and is succeeded by Ali Hassan Mwinyi, who begins liberalizing the socialist economy.
Constitutional reforms legalize multiparty politics, ending the long era of one-party rule under CCM.
Benjamin Mkapa wins Tanzania's first multiparty presidential election, accelerating economic reform and privatization.
Al-Qaeda bombs the U.S. embassy in Dar es Salaam in a coordinated attack with the Nairobi embassy bombing, killing several people.
Julius Nyerere, the revered founding father known as Mwalimu, dies and is mourned across the nation and Africa.
Tanzania becomes a founding member of the revived East African Community alongside Kenya and Uganda.
Jakaya Kikwete is elected president, presiding over a decade of economic growth and infrastructure expansion.
John Magufuli wins the presidency, launching anti-corruption and austerity campaigns that earn him the nickname 'the Bulldozer'.
President John Magufuli dies in office and is succeeded by Samia Suluhu Hassan, who becomes Tanzania's first female president.
